explanations.7: The syntax ${APP_HOME:-/app} means 'use the APP_HOME variable if it exists, otherwise use /app as a fallback default'.
explanations.8: This sets the numeric user ID inside the container; LOCAL_UID is read from your host machine's environment, falling back to 1000 if not set.
explanations.9: Similar to the user ID, this sets the group ID inside the container, using LOCAL_GID from your environment or 1000 as the default.
explanations.10: This defines the username that will own files in the container, reading LOCAL_USER from your environment or defaulting to 'vscode'.
explanations.13: The 5679 port is used by Debugpy (a Python debugger) to communicate between your IDE and the running code inside the container.
explanations.18: The dot (.) represents your current local directory, and it's being mounted (made accessible) inside the container at the APP_HOME path.
explanations.19: This creates a special named volume that persists VS Code extensions between container restarts instead of re-downloading them each time.
explanations.20: Your bash command history file is being mounted with read-write (rw) permissions so your shell history is saved between container sessions.
explanations.22: The 'sleep infinity' command keeps the container running indefinitely instead of exiting, which allows you to connect and work inside it.
explanations.24: This top-level 'volumes' section defines named volumes that can be reused; 'vscode-extensions' is referenced above on line 19.
